title: 10 Best Places to Sell Stuff Online in 2025 (Ranked by Value)
metaDescription: We ranked the 10 best places to sell stuff online in 2025 by fees, reach, ease, and real seller value. Spoiler: #1 gives you $40 just for signing up.
heroImageAlt: An overhead view of various items including electronics, clothing, sports gear, and home goods spread on a table, ready to be photographed for online sale
content:
10 Best Places to Sell Stuff Online in 2025 (Ranked by Value)

Every year, Americans leave billions of dollars sitting in garages, closets, and storage units — items they don't use, don't need, and could turn into real money in an afternoon. The question is never whether to sell; it's where. The right platform can mean the difference between pocketing $200 on an old laptop or $140 after fees. This is our definitive 2025 ranking of the best places to sell stuff online, evaluated on fees, privacy, flexibility, and overall seller value.

All 10 Platforms: Quick Comparison

Platform Fees Currency Local Pickup Ships Privacy Verdict
Swapedly 10% (max $20) Swap Bucks (1 SB = $1) Yes Yes No social login required 🏆 Best Overall
Facebook Marketplace 5% shipped / free local Cash / card Yes Yes FB profile required Best local reach
eBay 13.25% Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for rare items
Craigslist Free Cash Yes No Email anonymization Best free local option
Mercari 10% Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Easiest to start
Poshmark $2.95 / 20% Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for fashion community
OfferUp 12.9% min $1.99 Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Best local app design
Etsy 6.5% + listing Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for handmade/vintage
Amazon 8-15%+ Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for new goods at volume
Nextdoor Free Cash Yes No Neighborhood-verified Best for neighbors

#1 Swapedly — The Best Place to Sell Stuff Online in 2025

Swapedly earns the top spot by solving problems that every other platform on this list just accepts as normal. Cash handling? Gone — replaced by Swap Bucks (SB), a platform currency where 1 SB = $1 in value. High fees on valuable items? Capped — the 10% fee maxes out at $20, meaning a $500 item costs you the same in fees as a $200 item. Privacy concerns? Addressed — no Facebook account, no social login, no cross-platform data sharing required.

The $20 fee cap deserves a standalone moment. Sell a $1,000 piece of furniture on eBay: you pay $132.50. Sell it on Swapedly: you pay $20. That's $112.50 you keep. Over several high-value sales, the difference is not trivial — it's transformative.

The Swap Bucks system is smart in a way that grows on you. Every sale produces SB you spend on the platform. Sell a $200 camera, earn 180 SB after the fee, and immediately browse for a new lens, a piece of furniture, or anything else listed. There's no bank transfer to wait for, no PayPal seven-day hold — your proceeds are available the moment a sale closes.

New users get a $40 Swap Bucks gift card free — 40 SB to spend immediately on any listing. Claim yours at swapedly.com. That welcome bonus is the most generous introductory offer of any platform on this list. No strings attached.

Sellers can offer local pickup, shipping, or both — seller's choice. For those who sell frequently, Swapedly Plus at $9.99/month delivers 1.5x SB on every sale and 5 featured listings per month. The referral program adds 1 SB per friend who joins. A free listing structure means there's no cost to simply try it.

Swapedly Pros

  • Best fee structure: 10% capped at $20
  • $40 Swap Bucks free for new users
  • No cash handling — Swap Bucks are cleaner and safer
  • Privacy-first: no social media account required
  • Local pickup and shipping both supported
  • Swap Bucks immediately usable after every sale
  • Referral bonuses and Plus membership add value over time

Swapedly Cons

  • Swap Bucks can't be withdrawn as cash
  • Smaller absolute listing volume than eBay or Facebook Marketplace

#2 Facebook Marketplace — Best for Local Volume

No platform matches Facebook Marketplace for sheer local buyer density. In any US city, there are more active buyers scrolling Facebook Marketplace daily than any other local platform. For large items that can't economically ship — furniture, exercise equipment, appliances — that local audience is invaluable.

Free local transactions are a genuine draw. The 5% shipped fee is below most competitors. The tradeoffs are real: your Facebook identity is on full display, scam accounts are rampant, and cash-only local deals mean you're on your own if something goes wrong. For low-value items where the risk is manageable, Facebook Marketplace is a solid free option. For valuable items, protect yourself with a platform that has actual transaction coverage.

Facebook Marketplace Pros

  • Largest local buyer audience in the US
  • Free for local transactions
  • 5% shipped fee — competitive rate

Facebook Marketplace Cons

  • Your real Facebook profile is visible to all buyers
  • Scams are everywhere
  • Local deals have zero protection

#3 eBay — Best for Rare, Collectible, and Specialty Items

eBay has been around for 30 years, and it's still the definitive destination for anything rare, collectible, or specialty. The global buyer pool is unmatched. The auction format is still effective for items where you want the market to set the price. And the authentication programs for watches, sneakers, and trading cards give buyers confidence that commands higher prices.

The 13.25% fee is the real cost of that reach. On a $300 sale, that's $39.75 — nearly double what Swapedly charges. For rare items where eBay's audience pays prices that more than compensate for the fee premium, it's worth it. For everyday secondhand goods, it's not.

eBay Pros

  • Global buyer reach — no one else comes close
  • Auction format for price discovery
  • Authentication programs for premium categories

eBay Cons

  • 13.25% fee — most expensive on this list
  • Complex platform with steep learning curve
  • Returns skew heavily toward buyers

#4 Craigslist — Best Free Option for Large Local Items

Thirty years in and Craigslist is still standing on pure stubbornness and zero fees. For items like cars, furniture, appliances, and real estate, it draws serious local buyers who've been using the platform for decades. Zero fees means zero reasons to use anything else when a local cash deal is all you need.

Everything else about Craigslist is a compromise. The interface is embarrassingly dated, scam listings are abundant, there's no buyer or seller protection, and the mobile app experience is poor. It's a tool, not a platform — useful in specific contexts, painful in others.

Craigslist Pros

  • Completely free — no fees at all
  • Great for vehicles, housing, large furniture
  • Decades of buyer trust in major cities

Craigslist Cons

  • No buyer or seller protection
  • Terrible interface and app experience
  • High scam rate in many categories

#5 Mercari — The Easiest Platform to Start Selling

If you've never sold online before and want the gentlest possible introduction, Mercari is it. The app holds your hand through the listing process, the shipping integration removes the logistics mystery, and the flat 10% fee is transparently predictable. It's beginner-friendly in a way that eBay and Amazon simply are not.

The absence of a fee cap becomes relevant once you're selling items over $200. Beyond that threshold, Swapedly's structure is better. But for a first-time seller or someone with mostly lower-priced items, Mercari is a comfortable home base.

Mercari Pros

  • Best onboarding experience for new sellers
  • Flat 10% fee — easy to predict
  • Good shipping integration

Mercari Cons

  • No fee cap — gets costly on higher-value items
  • Buyer service fees complicate pricing
  • Limited differentiation above other platforms

#6 Poshmark — Best for Fashion Community Sellers

Poshmark's engaged fashion community is a real competitive advantage for sellers whose inventory is predominantly clothing, shoes, and accessories. The social sharing mechanics — Posh Parties, follower systems, sharing loops — drive organic visibility that you can't buy on most other platforms.

The 20% commission above $15 is genuinely painful, but regular Poshmark sellers often offset it with higher sell-through rates driven by that community engagement. The platform rewards active participation. If you're not willing to work the community mechanics, the fees become harder to justify.

Poshmark Pros

  • Strong community drives discovery
  • Fashion-specific audience with purchase intent
  • Simple shipping through Poshmark's label system

Poshmark Cons

  • 20% fee — highest on this list
  • Fashion only — limited for general goods
  • Requires active community participation

#7 OfferUp — Best Local Marketplace App

OfferUp is the best-designed local marketplace app in 2025 — clean interface, good location browsing, TruYou verification for safer local transactions. Since absorbing Letgo in 2020, it has a strong combined user base in most US cities. For sellers who want an app that looks and feels modern for local cash deals, OfferUp is the choice.

The 12.9% fee on shipped items is a stumbling block. It's higher than Mercari, higher than Swapedly, and significantly higher than Facebook Marketplace's 5%. For shipped sales, there are better options; for local transactions where the design quality matters, OfferUp is genuinely good.

OfferUp Pros

  • Best-designed local marketplace app
  • Large local user base post-Letgo merger
  • TruYou verification option

OfferUp Cons

  • 12.9% shipping fee — too high relative to alternatives
  • Cash local deals have no transaction protection
  • Higher fees than most platforms for shipped items

#8 Etsy — Best for Handmade and Vintage Sellers

Etsy's built-in audience of buyers actively seeking handmade goods, vintage items, and craft supplies is something no other platform on this list can replicate. If your inventory fits that category, Etsy's 6.5% transaction fee — lower than eBay or Poshmark — comes with the added value of targeted buyer traffic that has real purchase intent.

The $0.20 listing fee per item, required renewals, and increasingly competitive landscape in popular categories are real friction points. Etsy is best for sellers building a sustainable niche shop, not for casual one-off sellers clearing out a house.

Etsy Pros

  • Dedicated buyer audience for handmade/vintage
  • 6.5% fee is lower than eBay and Poshmark
  • Shop reviews build brand trust over time

Etsy Cons

  • $0.20 listing fee per item, renewed every 4 months
  • Crowded in popular categories
  • Not suitable for general used goods

#9 Amazon — Best for New Goods at Volume

Amazon's marketplace belongs on this list because for professional sellers with new goods, established brands, or UPC-coded products, nothing offers comparable traffic. But the fee complexity (8-15% referral fees, plus FBA fees, plus subscription costs) and the reality that Amazon competes against its own third-party sellers make it a tough environment for anyone without a serious strategy.

For casual sellers, secondhand goods, or unique handmade items, Amazon is the wrong tool. For businesses selling branded new products at volume with professional fulfillment? It's still unmatched.

Amazon Pros

  • Unparalleled traffic for new goods
  • FBA handles fulfillment at scale
  • Buyer trust is the highest of any platform

Amazon Cons

  • Complex, layered fee structure
  • Amazon competes directly against its own sellers
  • Not designed for secondhand or casual sellers

#10 Nextdoor — Best for Neighborhood Transactions

Nextdoor Marketplace does one thing exceptionally well: connect you with verified neighbors for local cash transactions. The trust level is high, the community feel is genuine, and for items that are truly neighborhood-appropriate — garden tools, baby gear, patio furniture — it works beautifully.

It earns last place on this list not because it's bad, but because its strengths are so specific and its limitations so significant. No shipping, no payment processing, no reach beyond your neighborhood. As a complement to other platforms, it's useful. As your only selling platform, it's severely limiting.

Nextdoor Pros

  • Neighborhood-verified accounts — maximum trust
  • Zero fees for local transactions
  • Community feel that other platforms can't replicate

Nextdoor Cons

  • No shipping capability whatsoever
  • Limited to immediate neighborhood area
  • Cash only — no payment protection

The Smart Seller's 2025 Strategy

The honest answer is that no single platform dominates every situation. A practical strategy for 2025 looks something like this:

Swapedly as your primary platform for most secondhand goods — the fee cap saves real money on anything over $200, the Swap Bucks keep your proceeds productive, and the privacy-first approach means you're not handing your social media identity to every buyer.

Facebook Marketplace or Nextdoor as a complement for large local items where shipping is impractical and cash-in-hand is fine.

eBay for rare, collectible, or specialty items that need the global auction audience to achieve their true market value.

Etsy if you're a maker or vintage seller building a niche brand over time.

And start with Swapedly — because the $40 Swap Bucks gift card for new users is 40 reasons to sign up today rather than tomorrow. Claim yours at swapedly.com.
